content.text: Pinecone is a fully managed, cloud-native vector database designed specifically for similarity search on high-dimensional embeddings.  Unlike self-hosted solutions such as Weaviate or Qdrant, Pinecone abstracts away all infrastructure: you interact with a REST or gRPC API, and Pinecone handles provisioning, indexing, replication, and scaling.  This makes it the fastest path from an embedding model to a production vector search service, at the cost of less control over the underlying index configuration.
Pinecone launched in 2021 as a purpose-built vector database.  Its defining characteristics:
no servers to provision, no FAISS builds, no index files to manage.  Create an index through the console or SDK and start upserting.
serverless indexes scale to zero and charge per query/storage; pod-based indexes run on dedicated compute for predictable latency.
a single index can be partitioned into isolated logical namespaces, enabling multi-tenant applications without separate indexes per tenant.
every vector can carry a JSON-like metadata payload; queries can filter by metadata fields server-side before returning results.
combines dense embedding similarity with sparse keyword matching (BM25-style) in a single query, improving retrieval for keyword-heavy domains.

expose the underlying ANN algorithm or allow custom distance metric implementations.  The service supports cosine similarity, dot product (inner product), and Euclidean distance.  All index internals are opaque and managed by Pinecone.

attrs.alt: Pinecone architecture showing serverless index (auto-scaling, distributed object store, namespaces) vs pod-based index (fixed compute pods with HNSW in-memory, shard router, replicas), and the 5-step query flow from embedding to LLM prompt

content.text: Serverless indexes (launched 2024) separate storage from compute.  Vectors are stored in a distributed object store (similar to S3 semantics) and compute is provisioned on demand per query.  When no queries arrive, compute scales to zero and you pay only for storage.
Serverless is billed on three dimensions:
Write units (WU) per upsert: proportional to the number of vectors and dimensionality.
Read units (RU) per query: proportional to the number of results and complexity of metadata filters.

Latency for serverless is typically higher than pod-based (tens of milliseconds vs single-digit milliseconds) because of cold-start overhead and the storage-compute separation.  Serverless is best suited for development, low-QPS applications, and workloads with unpredictable or spiky traffic.
Pod-based indexes run on dedicated hardware pods that Pinecone manages.  The index lives in memory on the pods, giving predictable sub-10ms p99 latency at high QPS.  Pod types are tiered:
Each pod type can be horizontally scaled by adding more pods (sharding) and vertically scaled by adding replicas (read load balancing).  Total capacity is approximately pods x vectors-per-pod.

from pinecone import Pinecone, ServerlessSpec

# Initialise client (reads PINECONE_API_KEY from env if not provided)
pc = Pinecone(api_key='YOUR_API_KEY')

# Create a serverless index (us-east-1, cosine similarity, 1536 dims for text-embedding-3-small)
if 'my-rag-index' not in pc.list_indexes().names():
    pc.create_index(
        name='my-rag-index',
        dimension=1536,
        metric='cosine',
        spec=ServerlessSpec(
            cloud='aws',
            region='us-east-1'
        )
    )

# Connect to the index
index = pc.Index('my-rag-index')
print(index.describe_index_stats())
# {'dimension': 1536, 'index_fullness': 0.0, 'total_vector_count': 0, ...}

from pinecone import PodSpec

pc.create_index(
    name='my-pod-index',
    dimension=1536,
    metric='cosine',
    spec=PodSpec(
        environment='us-east1-gcp',  # legacy region format for pods
        pod_type='p1.x1',            # p1 pod, 1x (no replication)
        pods=2,                      # 2 shards
        replicas=1,
    )
)
Vectors are upserted (inserted or replaced by ID) in batches.  Each vector is a tuple of
is a list of floats of length
is an optional dict of filterable fields.

import openai
from pinecone import Pinecone

pc = Pinecone()
index = pc.Index('my-rag-index')
client = openai.OpenAI()

documents = [
    {'id': 'doc-1', 'text': 'HNSW is a graph-based ANN algorithm.',
     'source': 'ml-book', 'chapter': 3},
    {'id': 'doc-2', 'text': 'Product quantization compresses embeddings.',
     'source': 'ml-book', 'chapter': 4},
    {'id': 'doc-3', 'text': 'Pinecone is a managed vector database.',
     'source': 'pinecone-docs', 'chapter': 1},
]

def embed_batch(texts):
    resp = client.embeddings.create(model='text-embedding-3-small', input=texts)
    return [item.embedding for item in resp.data]

BATCH = 100  # Pinecone recommends batches of 100 vectors
for start in range(0, len(documents), BATCH):
    batch = documents[start:start + BATCH]
    texts = [d['text'] for d in batch]
    embeddings = embed_batch(texts)
    vectors = [
        {
            'id': doc['id'],
            'values': emb,
            'metadata': {'source': doc['source'], 'chapter': doc['chapter'],
                         'text': doc['text']},  # store text for retrieval
        }
        for doc, emb in zip(batch, embeddings)
    ]
    index.upsert(vectors=vectors, namespace='default')

print(index.describe_index_stats())
# {'namespaces': {'default': {'vector_count': 3}}, 'total_vector_count': 3}

Pinecone recommends upserting in batches of 100 vectors.  Batches above 1,000 can trigger request-size limits.  For large corpora (millions of vectors), run upserts in parallel threads or async tasks, each writing its own batch.
A query takes a vector (or a vector ID for similarity re-ranking) and returns the top-K most similar vectors with their IDs, scores, and optionally metadata.

import openai
from pinecone import Pinecone

pc = Pinecone()
index = pc.Index('my-rag-index')
client = openai.OpenAI()

def search(query_text, top_k=5, namespace='default', filter_dict=None):
    # Embed the query
    resp = client.embeddings.create(
        model='text-embedding-3-small',
        input=[query_text]
    )
    query_vec = resp.data[0].embedding

    # Query Pinecone
    results = index.query(
        vector=query_vec,
        top_k=top_k,
        namespace=namespace,
        filter=filter_dict,       # optional metadata filter
        include_metadata=True,    # return stored metadata
        include_values=False,     # omit raw vectors for bandwidth
    )

    return [
        {'id': m.id, 'score': m.score, 'text': m.metadata.get('text', '')}
        for m in results.matches
    ]

hits = search('What is product quantization?', top_k=3)
for h in hits:
    print(f"{h['score']:.4f}  {h['id']}  {h['text'][:60]}")
# 0.8821  doc-2  Product quantization compresses embeddings.
# 0.7412  doc-1  HNSW is a graph-based ANN algorithm.
Metadata filters are applied server-side as a pre-filter or post-filter depending on the index type.  They use a MongoDB-style query syntax:

# Only return vectors from 'ml-book', chapter >= 3
results = index.query(
    vector=query_vec,
    top_k=10,
    filter={
        'source': {'$eq': 'ml-book'},
        'chapter': {'$gte': 3},
    },
    include_metadata=True,
)

# Supported filter operators:
# $eq, $ne       -- equality / inequality
# $gt, $gte      -- numeric greater than
# $lt, $lte      -- numeric less than
# $in, $nin      -- value in / not in list
# $and, $or      -- logical combination

# Example: multi-field OR filter
results = index.query(
    vector=query_vec,
    top_k=5,
    filter={
        '$or': [
            {'source': {'$eq': 'pinecone-docs'}},
            {'chapter': {'$in': [1, 2]}},
        ]
    },
    include_metadata=True,
)
Metadata fields used in filters must be declared at index creation time on pod-based indexes (via
) to be indexed efficiently.  On serverless indexes, all metadata fields are automatically indexed.  Filtering on un-indexed fields on pod-based indexes triggers a slow full-scan fallback.

Heavy metadata filtering reduces the effective candidate pool and can degrade recall because the ANN graph was built without knowledge of the filter.  For very selective filters (< 1% of corpus matches), consider using separate namespaces instead of metadata to keep the candidate pool dense.
A namespace is a logical partition within an index.  Upserts and queries scoped to a namespace cannot see vectors in other namespaces, providing tenant isolation without separate indexes.

# Upsert into tenant-specific namespaces
index.upsert(vectors=tenant_a_vectors, namespace='tenant-a')
index.upsert(vectors=tenant_b_vectors, namespace='tenant-b')

# Query is automatically scoped -- tenant B cannot see tenant A's data
results_a = index.query(vector=query_vec, top_k=5, namespace='tenant-a')
results_b = index.query(vector=query_vec, top_k=5, namespace='tenant-b')

# Describe per-namespace stats
stats = index.describe_index_stats()
for ns, info in stats['namespaces'].items():
    print(f'{ns}: {info["vector_count"]} vectors')
# tenant-a: 10000 vectors
# tenant-b: 5000 vectors

# Delete all vectors in a namespace
index.delete(delete_all=True, namespace='tenant-a')

Namespace names are arbitrary strings; they are created implicitly on first upsert and deleted when their last vector is removed.  There is no limit on the number of namespaces per index on serverless; pod-based indexes share the same capacity across all namespaces.

Pinecone supports hybrid search by combining dense ANN similarity with sparse vector scores in a single query.  Sparse vectors typically represent TF-IDF or BM25 weights over a vocabulary, capturing lexical matching that dense embeddings miss.

from pinecone_text.sparse import BM25Encoder

# Fit BM25 on the corpus
bm25 = BM25Encoder()
bm25.fit([doc['text'] for doc in documents])

# Upsert with both dense and sparse vectors
vectors = []
for doc, dense_emb in zip(documents, embeddings):
    sparse_emb = bm25.encode_documents([doc['text']])[0]
    vectors.append({
        'id': doc['id'],
        'values': dense_emb,          # dense: float list
        'sparse_values': sparse_emb,  # sparse: {'indices': [...], 'values': [...]}
        'metadata': {'text': doc['text']},
    })
index.upsert(vectors=vectors)

# Query with hybrid scores
query_text = 'vector compression'
dense_q = embed_batch([query_text])[0]
sparse_q = bm25.encode_queries([query_text])[0]

# alpha controls dense/sparse balance: 1.0=pure dense, 0.0=pure sparse
alpha = 0.75
results = index.query(
    vector=dense_q,
    sparse_vector=sparse_q,
    top_k=5,
    alpha=alpha,
    include_metadata=True,
)
Hybrid search is available on serverless indexes and select pod types.  The
parameter linearly interpolates between dense and sparse scores: values around 0.7-0.8 typically outperform either mode alone in benchmarks on mixed natural-language retrieval tasks.
The canonical retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pattern with Pinecone:

import openai
from pinecone import Pinecone

pc = Pinecone()
index = pc.Index('my-rag-index')
client = openai.OpenAI()

def rag_query(user_question, top_k=5):
    # Step 1: embed the question
    q_resp = client.embeddings.create(
        model='text-embedding-3-small',
        input=[user_question]
    )
    q_vec = q_resp.data[0].embedding

    # Step 2: retrieve top-K relevant chunks from Pinecone
    matches = index.query(
        vector=q_vec,
        top_k=top_k,
        include_metadata=True,
    ).matches

    # Step 3: build context from retrieved chunks
    context = '
'.join(
        f"[{m.score:.2f}] {m.metadata['text']}"
        for m in matches
        if m.score > 0.70  # minimum relevance threshold
    )

    if not context:
        return 'No relevant documents found.'

    # Step 4: generate answer with retrieved context
    completion = client.chat.completions.create(
        model='gpt-4o-mini',
        messages=[
            {'role': 'system',
             'content': 'Answer using only the provided context.'},
            {'role': 'user',
             'content': f'Context:
{context}

Question: {user_question}'},
        ]
    )
    return completion.choices[0].message.content

print(rag_query('How does product quantization reduce memory usage?'))
For production RAG systems at scale, prefer pod-based indexes with
pods.  Add replicas to spread read load and add pods (shards) to increase total capacity.  Monitor the
; indexes above 80% fullness show degraded performance.
Latency on serverless can be improved by pinning the connection and keeping it warm with periodic no-op queries, or by upgrading to a paid tier that reserves capacity.
Pinecone uses upsert semantics -- upserting a vector with an existing ID replaces it in-place.  Deletion by ID is available on all index types:

# Delete specific vectors by ID
index.delete(ids=['doc-1', 'doc-3'], namespace='default')

# Delete by metadata filter (pod-based only)
index.delete(
    filter={'source': {'$eq': 'outdated-source'}},
    namespace='default',
)

# Fetch a vector by ID (for inspection / debugging)
result = index.fetch(ids=['doc-2'], namespace='default')
print(result['vectors']['doc-2']['metadata'])

# Update only metadata (not the vector values)
index.update(
    id='doc-2',
    set_metadata={'chapter': 5, 'revised': True},
    namespace='default',
)

There is no in-place update of vector values -- to change a vector's embedding, upsert a new record with the same ID.  Deletion on serverless is eventually consistent (may take a few seconds to be reflected in query results).
Mismatching embedding model and index dimension.

The index dimension is fixed at creation.  If you later switch from text-embedding-ada-002 (1536 dims) to text-embedding-3-large (3072 dims), you must create a new index and re-embed the entire corpus.
Metadata is included in every query response if include_metadata=True.  Store a chunk ID in metadata and look up full text in a separate store (Postgres, S3) to avoid inflated response sizes.
Upserting one vector at a time causes excessive API round-trips.  Batch in groups of 100 and parallelise across threads.
Pod-based indexes degrade when full.  Check describe_index_stats() and add pods before reaching 80% capacity.
Using metadata filters as the primary retrieval strategy.
Filters on low-selectivity fields (e.g. 'language=en' on a mostly-English corpus) add overhead without benefit.  Use filters only for high-selectivity conditions.
Chunk documents to 200-500 tokens before embedding.  Pinecone stores one vector per chunk; oversized chunks produce diluted embeddings that hurt recall.

Store the chunk text in metadata (up to ~40KB per vector) so that retrieval is self-contained.  For longer texts, store only a short preview in metadata and keep the full text in a linked document store.
Use namespaces instead of separate indexes for multi-tenant workloads -- namespaces share pods, reducing cost while maintaining isolation.
Set a minimum score threshold (e.g. 0.70 for cosine similarity) in your query results handler to avoid injecting irrelevant context into the LLM prompt.
For pod-based indexes in production, set replicas >= 2 to survive a pod failure without downtime.
Use the Pinecone gRPC client (pinecone[grpc]) instead of the default REST client in latency-sensitive paths -- gRPC reduces per-query overhead by ~20%.
Pinecone is a fully managed vector database with a REST/gRPC API -- no infrastructure to operate.  Choose serverless for development or bursty workloads, pod-based for production SLA requirements.
Every vector carries an ID, float values (the embedding), and an optional metadata dict.  Upsert semantics mean re-inserting with the same ID updates in-place.
Namespaces partition an index for multi-tenancy without separate indexes.  Metadata filters apply server-side MongoDB-style predicates to restrict the candidate pool.
Hybrid search combines dense ANN similarity and sparse BM25 scores; alpha controls the mix.  Values around 0.75 typically outperform pure dense retrieval on mixed corpora.
For RAG, query with top_k=5-20, filter on a minimum score threshold, and pass retrieved chunk texts as context to the LLM in a single prompt.
Monitor index_fullness on pod-based indexes; add pods before reaching 80% to avoid performance degradation.
